topic: budget meal prep ideas — student budget

turns.content: man i need some help with meal prep ideas because im spending way too much on food rn and my bank account is looking tragic
Happy to help with that. What kind of budget are we working with per week, and do you have any dietary preferences or things you won't eat?

like £25-30 a week max tbh. im a student so its tight. no dietary restrictions i eat everything but i grew up on caribbean food so i like flavour you know. cant be eating plain chicken and rice five days a week i will lose my mind

That's very doable with some planning. Caribbean flavours actually work brilliantly for budget meal prep because the base ingredients are cheap -- rice, dried beans, lentils, root vegetables, tinned coconut milk. Here are a few ideas:

1. **Big batch curry** -- chickpeas or red lentils with coconut milk, scotch bonnet, and whatever veg is on offer. Lasts 4-5 portions.
2. **Jerk-seasoned chicken thighs** -- thighs are much cheaper than breast, marinate overnight, bake in bulk.
3. **Rice and peas** -- a huge pot costs almost nothing. Kidney beans, coconut milk, thyme, allspice.
4. **Sweet potato and black bean stew** -- hearty, filling, freezes well.

The trick is cooking two or three big batches on a Sunday and portioning them out. Want me to break down a sample weekly plan?
yes please that would be proper helpful. and yeah chicken thighs are the one init. my nan always said only people who dont know how to cook buy chicken breast 😂 thighs have all the flavour

Your nan knows what she's talking about. Here's a sample week on about £25-28:

**Sunday cook-up (1-2 hours):**
- Batch 1: Jerk chicken thighs (1kg pack, around £3) with rice and peas
- Batch 2: Lentil and sweet potato coconut curry
- Snack prep: Boil a dozen eggs, chop fruit

**Monday-Wednesday:** Jerk chicken + rice and peas for lunch, curry for dinner (or swap)

**Thursday:** Stir-fry whatever veg is left with egg noodles (noodles are about 20p a pack)

**Friday-Saturday:** Black bean quesadillas or wraps using tinned black beans, cheese, and hot sauce. Cheap and quick.

**Breakfast all week:** Porridge with banana (oats are about 75p for a big bag)

**Shopping list rough cost:**
- Chicken thighs: £3
- Rice (2kg): £2
- Kidney beans + lentils + black beans (tinned): £3
- Coconut milk x2: £2
- Sweet potatoes + onions + peppers: £4
- Eggs (12): £2
- Oats + bananas: £2
- Noodles + wraps + cheese: £4
- Spices (if you already have basics): £0

That's about £22, leaving a few quid for extras.

this is actually sick tbh. i already have bare spices at home because my nan keeps giving me stuff from her cupboard like she thinks im going to starve haha. scotch bonnet allspice thyme paprika ive got all that

Then you're already ahead of the game. Spices are the expensive bit when you're starting from scratch, so having those stocked means your weekly shop stays well under £25. A couple more tips:

- **Aldi or Lidl** for the basics -- rice, beans, coconut milk, veg. Significantly cheaper than the big supermarkets.
- **Freeze portions in containers** so you're not eating the same thing four days straight. Alternate between the jerk chicken and the curry.
- **Yellow sticker shopping** in the evenings -- most supermarkets reduce meat and veg after 7pm. You can sometimes get chicken thighs for half price and freeze them.

With the spice collection you've got, you can make budget food taste like it cost three times as much.

ngl the yellow sticker tip is elite i already do that sometimes at the tesco near me. you catch some proper bargains if you time it right. one time i got a whole chicken for like £2.50 and made it last three days. jerk roast chicken then pulled the rest off for wraps then boiled the carcass for soup. my nan would be proud of that one

That's textbook budget cooking right there -- getting three meals out of one chicken is exactly the move. The soup from the carcass is chef-level resourcefulness. Sounds like you've already got good instincts, you just need a bit more structure with the weekly plan so you're not improvising every day and ending up buying takeaway when you're tired.

thats literally what happens tho 😂 ill have a long day and cant be bothered to cook so i order something and thats like £8-10 gone just like that. if i had stuff prepped and ready to microwave id save bare money. alright im doing this. sunday cook-up is becoming a thing. appreciate the help bruv

That's the plan. Once you get into the routine, it becomes second nature. The first couple of Sundays might feel like effort but after that it's just what you do. And you'll notice the difference in your bank account by the end of the month. Good luck with it -- your nan's spice cupboard is carrying you.
